note_en:
Welcome to Edition 3.05 of the Rocket Report! This week, much of the focus is on small rockets, and we have plenty of new deals to discuss. So let's jump right into it!
 As always, we  welcome reader submissions, and if you don't want to miss an issue, please subscribe using the box below (the form will not appear on AMP-enabled versions of the site). Each report will include information on small-, medium-, and heavy-lift rockets as well as a quick look ahead at the next three launches on the calendar.
  NASA ready to buy suborbital rides for its people. This week, NASA formally asked the US space industry to dish the details on its plans for brief spaceflights. In essence, the space agency said it wants to buy brief hops into space for its Astronaut Corps and scientists, but it needs more information,  Ars reports. NASA Administrator Jim Bridenstine said the program seeks mostly to increase the time NASA spends in microgravity.
 Balancing cost and risk ... The biggest question concerns the risk that NASA is willing to accept in putting its people on these space vehicles. For the space shuttle program, NASA had complete oversight of the vehicle's development. Although the commercial crew program was a public-private partnership, NASA still had significant insight into every facet of SpaceX's Crew Dragon and Boeing's Starliner spacecraft because it paid for most of the development costs. Now, NASA is solely a customer. "We're not going to make it more dangerous than orbital flight," Bridenstine said of suborbital flight.
 Iridium inks deal for up to six launches with Relativity. Relativity Space says it has reached an agreement with Iridium to launch six of the company's next-generation communications satellites,  Ars reports. Each of the Iridium NEXT satellites, which weigh 850kg, will be launched individually on Relativity's Terran 1 rocket. This means the new Launch Services Agreement will lead to as many as six Terran 1 missions, beginning no earlier than 2023.
 Needs a West Coast launch site ... That's because these are backup satellites to a constellation of 66 satellites in a near-polar orbit, spread across six different planes at an altitude of 780km. To reach this orbit, the Terran 1 rocket would need to launch toward a pole, and so the company has reached a "Right of Entry" agreement to build a second launch site at Vandenberg Air Force Base in California. The company will do so at a site south of Space Launch Complex 6.
 The easiest way to keep up with Eric Berger's space reporting is to sign up for his newsletter, we'll collect his stories in your inbox.
 Defense Department supports small launch companies. Six space launch companies were selected to receive Defense Department contracts funded under the Defense Production Act to shore up domestic industries financially impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic,  SpaceNews reports. Aevum, Astra, X-Bow, Rocket Lab, Space Vector and VOX Space each will receive noncompetitive contracts to launch two rideshare missions for government customers over the next 24 months.
 Time to step up  ... Small launch—along with aircraft manufacturing and shipbuilding—was one of the sectors that the Pentagon identified as most affected by the financial downturn caused by the pandemic. This is a welcome development, and it will be interesting to see which companies manage to deliver. (submitted by trimeta, platykurtic and JohnCarter17)
 Scott Pace urges caution on point-to-point travel. Coincidence or not, Virgin Galactic's stock has risen since the company began talking about its long-term strategy of developing point-to-point commercial suborbital flights late in 2019. The company's chairman, Chamath Palihapitiya, has said, "When you think about that world, that world will be five to 10 years away."
 Over the horizon ... However, Executive Secretary Scott Pace of the National Space Council this week raised doubts about that. "Maybe this is a poor reflection on me, but I still see that as somewhat speculative and somewhat over the horizon," he said,  according to Ars. "I think people look forward to the possibility of point-to-point passenger and cargo travel, but right now just getting routine suborbital access to space and pushing hard on unmanned hypersonics is where the action is."
 Firefly applies for September launch permit. Another prominent smallsat launch company may make its debut this fall.  Parabolic Arc reports that Firefly has applied for a permit to launch its Alpha booster from Vandenberg Air Force Base on September 6 or 7. With an advertised lift capacity of 1 ton to low Earth orbit, Alpha is among the bigger of the new smallsat rockets. This is likely a preliminary filing and not a formal launch date.
 Sounds like they're confident ... "Once the launch vehicle reaches the mission altitude of 300 km, it will deploy commercial payloads into orbit. The second stage will complete one full orbit while it downlinks data to VAFB and KSAT ground station locations in Hawaii, Mauritius, and South Africa," the company said in its application. (submitted by Ken the Bin)
 Scottish spaceport receives tentative approval. A proposal to build a spaceport on a northern peninsula in Scotland appears set to pass a key environmental hurdle. Advisors to the local government, Highland Council, have recommended that Space Hub Sutherland be approved,  the BBC reports. It is hoped the site will spur job creation in the area, which is suited for polar launches.
 More against than for ... The recommendation came even after the local authority received 457 objections to the plans and only 118 representations in support of them. Impact on the environment and risk to human health were among the reasons for the objections. By way of a compromise, council officials said launches should be limited to 12 per year to reduce the amount of plastic and metal debris falling into the sea during rocket launches. (submitted by BH and JohnCarter17)
 There's a new space-balloon company ... The space entrepreneurs who planned to send passengers ballooning into the stratosphere earlier this decade have revived the idea for a new venture called Space Perspective,  GeekWire reports. Co-CEOs Taber MacCallum and Jane Poynter unveiled their concept for a balloon-borne capsule called Spaceship Neptune this week, saying that uncrewed test flights are due to begin early next year.
 Price of poker has gone up ... Seven years ago, MacCallum and Poynter had a similar unveiling for World View Enterprises, an Arizona-based venture that aimed to fly people to an altitude of 100,000 feet. (The cost? A cool $75,000 a ticket.) Since 2013, World View has pivoted to sending up uncrewed payloads on balloon platforms known as Stratollites. Some of the plan's details are still up in the air, but Poynter said passenger flights are pegged to begin around 2024. She expects the price of a ticket to be in the range of $125,000. (submitted by Ken the Bin and JohnCarter17)
  ISRO invites private sector to play important role. Suggesting this is a "major reform," the chief of India's space agency, K. Sivan, announced new ways in which private industry will be able to explore in the space sector. The space organization said the private sector will be allowed to carry out space activities such as the building of rockets and satellites as well as providing launch services, the  Deccan Chronicle reports.
 Catching up to competitors ... The reforms include streamlining regulations and permits for private spaceflight activities. The goal is to not only enable accelerated growth of the space sector but also help the Indian industry play a major role in the global space economy. This seems to be an important step for Indian companies to catch up to private firms in the United States and, increasingly, China. (submitted by Ken the Bin and JohnCarter17)
 Launch of Perseverance slips two more days. The launch of NASA's Perseverance Mars rover and Ingenuity Mars Helicopter has been delayed two days to July 22 after an issue with ground-support equipment at the Kennedy Space Center,  Spaceflight Now reports. The problem held up encapsulation of the spacecraft inside the payload fairing of its Atlas 5 rocket.
 Still some flexibility ... NASA said the spacecraft and launch vehicle are healthy, and encapsulation of the rover is being completed this week inside a climate-controlled clean room where NASA has prepared numerous interplanetary missions for launch. Separately, ULA teams completed a countdown rehearsal on the Atlas 5 rocket, without its Mars-bound payload, on Monday at Cape Canaveral. The launch window to Mars closes in mid-August. (submitted by JohnCarter17)
 NASA OKs flying crew on used Falcon 9 boosters. NASA has agreed to allow its astronauts to fly on reused Crew Dragon spaceships and Falcon 9 boosters beginning as soon as SpaceX's third launch of a crew to the International Space Station,  Spaceflight Now reports. This mission is expected to launch next year.
 In NASA's best interests ... "SpaceX has proposed to reuse future Falcon 9 and/or Crew Dragon systems or components for NASA missions to the International Space Station because they believe it will be beneficial from a safety and/or cost standpoint," NASA spokesman Josh Finch said. "NASA performed an in-depth review and determined that the terms of the overall contract modification were in the best interests of the government." (submitted by Ken the Bin)
  SpaceX tests new metal alloy for Starship tanks. SpaceX this week tested the latest version of a pressure tank for the Starship vehicle to failure.  Teslarati reports that the primary purpose was to test a new steel alloy, 304L, that is supposed to have improved performance at cryogenic temperatures.
 Reaching for a higher bar ... Earlier this month, during its first cryogenic pressure test with liquid nitrogen, SpaceX CEO Elon Musk revealed that this so-called SN7 test tank managed to reach 7.6 bar before succumbing to a leak. The tank was subsequently repaired before the second test. Musk has not yet said at what pressure the tank failed during the second attempt. The company would like to reach 8.4 bar, a pressure deemed necessary to eventually human-rate Starship. (submitted by Ken the Bin)
 Will Stennis Space Center get a name change? On Wednesday, NASA announced that it is naming its headquarters building after Mary Jackson, the first Black woman engineer to work at the agency,  Space Policy Online reports. The decision comes as Virgin Orbit Vice President Will Pomerantz is pushing to rename Stennis Space Center. The rationale is simple: John C. Stennis was a racist, who was called "the heart, soul, and brains of the white supremacist caucus in the 1948 Congress." This is probably not someone NASA should be honoring.
 NASA noncommittal so far ... While he said there were a number of good choices, Pomerantz said perhaps the Mississippi center where rocket engines are tested should be renamed in honor of Mae Jemison, the first Black woman in space. In a statement, NASA responded that the agency is dedicated to advancing diversity but did not agree or disagree with the idea of renaming the center. Personally, I would also suggest Annie Easley, who played a role in the development of Centaur, arguably the most successful upper stage of all time, and is from neighboring Alabama.

这家美国公司的最新尝试被称为“智能商店”(Smart Stores)。在这个项目中，亚马逊为实体店提供了软件，以维护他们在商店中的库存的数字日志，并向他们提供二维码。
当消费者走到商店，用亚马逊应用程序扫描这个二维码时，他们会看到商店提供的一切，以及顾客的任何折扣和过去的评论。他们可以选择商品并使用Amazon Pay付款。印度的Amazon Pay支持一系列支付服务，包括广受欢迎的UPI，以及借记卡和信用卡。
亚马逊告诉TechCrunch，它在两个月前就试行了这个项目，在看到早期的吸引力后，现在正式推出了这个项目。该公司表示，从夫妻店到包括Big Bazaar，MedPlus在内的大型零售连锁店，以及更多的超市，已经有1万多家商店部署了该公司的系统。
该公司表示，这些“数字店面”对消费者和店主都是双赢的。消费者不需要呆在商店里担心处理塑料卡或现金-他们使用Amazon Pay也会获得奖励。
客户还可以使用亚马逊的稍后付款功能，该功能使他们能够分期付款。所有这一切都意味着商家的客流量增加了，销售额也在提高。亚马逊表示，它不会从商家或客户那里收取任何提成。
最近几个季度，该公司一直在积极与印度的实体店合作，利用它们在印度的巨大影响力来扩大其配送网络和仓库，甚至仅仅依靠它们的库存来推动销售。
该公司进军占印度绝大多数销售额的实体零售市场之际，Facebook、Flipkart、谷歌和Reliance Jio平台最近筹集了152亿美元，也在争先恐后地抢占这一市场。周四，谷歌表示，计划在今年年底前向印度的商家提供贷款。
这些夫妻店提供各式各样的商品，都是家庭式经营，工资很低，租金很低，甚至没有租金。由于它们无处不在(据行业估计，印度有超过3000万家社区商店)，没有零售巨头能提供更快的送货速度。最重要的是，他们的经济状况往往比大多数数字同行更好。
本地化是新的全球商业计划。在印度，#O2O将以比在线零售更快的速度成为主流。https://t.co/NHIeFr2nJ6。
亚马逊支付已经被数百万家当地商店接受，我们正试图通过Smart Stores让客户在当地商店的购买体验变得更加方便和安全。此外，通过EMIS、银行优惠和奖励，我们寻求使这些购买对客户来说更实惠和更有价值，并帮助增加商家的销售额。“。Amazon Pay首席执行官Mahendra Nerurkar在声明中称。
亚马逊对印度实体零售市场的兴趣虽然迟缓，但日益增长，这并不令人意外。该公司通常比大多数公司需要更长的时间来研究市场，然后增加自己的宣传来应对这些挑战。最近的另一个例子是：它进军印度的外卖市场。
尽管人们对实体零售市场普遍感兴趣，但有一件事还没有公司谈论，那就是他们计划如何从商业上激励这些商家。这些公司建立的技术解决方案无疑推动了它们的销售，但这些小企业中有相当一部分采取现金和少报收入的方式来节省税收。对于他们中的许多人来说，这种激励是任何其他激励的倍数。
note_en:
The American firm’s latest attempt is called “ Smart Stores.” For this program, Amazon is providing physical stores with software to maintain a digital log of the inventory they have in the shop, and supplying them with a QR code.
 When consumers walk to the store and scan this QR code with the Amazon app, they see everything the shop has to offer, as well as any discounts and past reviews from customers. They can select the items and pay for it using Amazon Pay. Amazon Pay in India supports a range of payments services including the popular UPI, and debit and credit cards.
  Amazon told TechCrunch that it piloted this project two months ago and is formally launching it now after seeing the early traction. More than 10,000 stores, ranging from mom and pop stores to big retail chains including Big Bazaar, MedPlus and More Supermarkets have deployed the company’s system, it said.
 The company said these “digital storefronts” are a win-win for both consumers and shop owners. Consumers do not need to stay inside the store and worry about handling plastic cards or cash — and they will also get rewards for using Amazon Pay.
  Customers also get the ability to use Amazon’s Pay Later feature that enables them to pay in installments. All of this means that merchants are increased footfalls and improving their sales. Amazon said it is not taking any cut from merchants or customers.
 The company has been aggressively engaging with physical stores in India in recent quarters, using their vast presence in the nation to  expand its delivery network and warehouses and even just  relying on their inventory to drive sales.
 The company’s push in the physical retails, which accounts for the vast majority of sales in India, comes as Facebook, Flipkart, Google, and Reliance Jio Platforms, which  recently raised $15.2 billion, also  race to capture this market. On Thursday, Google said  it plans to offer loans to merchants in India by the end of this year.
 These mom-and-pop stores offer all kinds of items, are family-run and pay low wages and little to no rent. Since they are ubiquitous (there are more than 30 million neighborhood stores in India, according to industry estimates), no retail giant can offer a faster delivery. And on top of that, their economics are often better than most of their digital counterparts.
 Local is new global business plan.  #O2O will be mainstream faster than "online retail" in India.  https://t.co/NHIeFr2nJ6
 “Amazon Pay is already accepted at millions of local shops, we are trying to make customers’ buying experience at local shops even more convenient and safe through Smart Stores. Further, through EMIs, bank offers and rewards, we seek to make these purchases more affordable and rewarding for customers, and help increase sales for merchants.” said Mahendra Nerurkar, chief executive of Amazon Pay, in a statement.
 Amazon’s tardy but increasingly growing interest in the Indian physical retails market is not surprising. The company often takes longer than most firms to study the market and then adds its own spin to tackle those challenges. Another recent case in point: Its  foray into food delivery market in India.
 Despite ubiquitous interest in the physical retails market, one thing that  that no company is talking about yet is just how they plan to commercially incentivize these merchants. The technology solutions built by these companies is unarguably driving sales for them, but a significant number of these small businesses take cash and under report their revenue to save tax. That incentive is multifold of any other incentive for many of them.
title: 亚马逊在印度推出“智能商店”赢得夫妻店
title_en: Amazon launches ‘Smart Stores’ in India to win mom and pop

note:
维珍澳大利亚航空的管理人员表示，他们已承诺将这家遭受重创的航空公司出售给美国私募股权集团贝恩资本(Bain Capital)。
上周五达成的这笔交易，将使债权人对该航空公司的未来几乎没有发言权，除非他们采取法律行动推翻该交易。
管理人员是会计师事务所德勤(Deloitte)的合伙人，他们表示，他们在周五上午与贝恩签署了一项协议，该协议将保留数千个工作岗位，并对航空公司进行资本重组。
如果交易得以进行，昆士兰最终还将获得该航空公司高达5%的股份，此前该公司同意提供约2亿美元的现金和税费减免-此举旨在防止该航空公司的总部在新南威尔士州的竞争报价下迁往悉尼。
由于工会周四公开支持这笔交易，剩下的最后一个绊脚石是一群欠下约20亿美元的债券持有人，他们在本周晚些时候提出了收购该航空公司的报价。
该组织的一名发言人抨击管理人员没有考虑这项提议，他表示，该组织将继续推动这一提议。
“否则，由于管理人今天的决定，贝恩提议的必然结果是给投资者留下一个非常糟糕的复苏，这显然是一个不公平的结果，”他说.。
首席行政官沃恩·斯特劳布里奇(Vaughan Strawbridge)告诉澳大利亚卫报(Guardian Australia)，出售过程于8周前开始，已经“创造了结果的确定性，不仅是为了出售，也是为了航空公司和就业机会”。
他表示，贝恩与竞争对手赛勒斯资本合伙公司(Cyrus Capital Partners)之间的谈判一直很困难，并驳斥了赛勒斯的指控，即管理人没有与之接触，也没有回复电话。
为了解决维珍澳大利亚航空迫在眉睫的现金危机，贝恩已同意向该航空公司提供1.25亿美元贷款，斯特劳布里奇表示，这笔钱应该足以让维珍澳大利亚航空度过8月份，届时债权人将举行会议。
但债权人(包括该航空公司1万名员工和欠下20亿美元的债券持有人)将没有发言权，无法决定出售过程是否会继续进行。
斯特劳布里奇说：“我们已经同意出售这项业务，我们有这种销售力。”
“我们一直说我们在寻找6月底前达成有约束力的协议，这就是我们所做的。
“如果我们不走上具有约束力的销售协议的道路，那么我们就不会为航空公司实现结果的确定性，也不会实现就业的确定性。”
他表示，债权人将在会上投票决定这笔交易是否通过一种名为公司安排契约的机制完成，但他补充称：“这不是我们完成交易的唯一途径”。
斯特劳布里奇拒绝透露贝恩总共将支付多少，新的维珍澳大利亚将保留多少工作岗位，以及债权人将获得多少68亿美元的欠款。
在此之前，竞争对手赛勒斯资本合伙公司(Cyrus Capital Partners)于周五上午戏剧性地退出了竞选，抨击管理人员未能与其接触并回复电话。
Cyrus表示，它上周提交了报价，但“自那以后，管理人员没有回复过电话、电子邮件，也没有与Cyrus进行有意义的接触，以推进其报价”。
该公司表示，在周四的竞标之后，又推出了一项更优惠的方案，将“提高交易价值，提高无担保债券持有人的回报，并为管理人提供更多确定性”。
赛勒斯说：“除了收到确认函外，这封信也没有收到任何回应。”
尽管提出了实质性的改进，但管理人员仍未与Cyrus就其报价进行接触。
斯特劳布里奇说，赛勒斯的团队是“一群伟大的人”。
他说：“在整个过程中，我们真的很喜欢与他们合作，我们花了很多时间与他们在一起。”
“我们四位行政人员和我们的顾问周二花了两个多小时与他们一起审阅他们的商业计划，审阅他们的提案。
“我对他们的评论感到失望--我理解他们的不安，我完全理解。”
此举还将债券持有人拒之门外，他们本周提出了一项最新提议，拟向该航空公司注入10亿美元，并将其在证券交易所重新上市。
斯特劳布里奇说，这项提议也经过了审查，但它是高度有条件的，不具约束力。
他们表示，这笔交易“支持保罗·斯库拉(Paul Scurrah)目前领导的管理团队，以及他们对航空公司的改进计划”，表明斯库拉至少在短期内将继续担任首席执行官。
在冠状病毒旅行限制大幅削减乘客数量，莫里森政府拒绝了其一再提出的纾困请求后，维珍澳大利亚航空于4月份崩溃进入破产管理程序。
斯特劳布里奇说：“贝恩资本对这项业务提出了强有力的、令人信服的报价，这将确保澳大利亚第二家航空公司、数千名员工及其家人的未来，并确保澳大利亚继续享受竞争激烈的航空业带来的好处。”
从我们被任命的第一天起，我们就专注于实现一家重组后的、可持续的和盈利的航空公司能够向所有利益相关者提供的可能的最佳结果。
“在短短八周多一点的时间里，这是一个非常积极的结果。我们当然对各方表现出的兴趣程度感到鼓舞，尽管当时的市场环境令人眼花缭乱，我们的最后两家集团是如何接近他们的出价的，以及对业务的支持是如何得到如此多方面的支持。“
选择贝恩资本作为优先竞标者对以运输工人工会(Transport Workers Union)为首的工会是一个打击，因为这家投资集团计划成立一家规模小于其他竞标者的航空公司，而且在美国有破坏工会的记录。
然而，TWU全国秘书迈克尔·凯恩(Michael Kaine)表示，“贝恩提出了一个坚实的报价，以确保行政官员的建议，我们很高兴与他们合作，制定让航空公司重新站稳脚跟的计划”。
他再次呼吁莫里森政府帮助遭受冠状病毒危机重创的航空业，引入一项“航空保管员”一揽子计划，以便在9月份按计划切断支付给工人的“工作保管员”工资的情况下，保持资金流动。
维珍澳大利亚航空(Virgin Australia)规模更大的竞争对手澳航(Qantas)周四裁员6000人，并宣布融资19亿美元。该公司告诉市场，国际航班至少在一年内不会恢复。
凯恩说：“维珍航空和澳航都需要政府的财政援助、支持和指导，以渡过未来几个月的困难时期，因为航空旅行举步维艰。”
note_en:
Virgin Australia’s administrators say they have committed to selling the stricken airline to US private equity group Bain Capital.
 The deal, agreed on Friday, would leave creditors owed $6.8bn with little say in the future of the airline unless they take legal action to overturn the deal.
 The administrators, partners at accounting firm Deloitte, said they signed a deal with Bain on Friday morning that would retain thousands of jobs and recapitalise the airline.
 Queensland will also end up with as much as 5% of the airline if the deal goes ahead after agreeing to tip in about $200m in cash and tax and fee breaks – a move that acts to prevent the airline’s headquarters shifting to Sydney under a rival offer from NSW.
 With unions publicly backing the deal on Thursday, the last remaining stumbling block is a group of bondholders owed about $2bn, who lobbed a late bid for the airline during the week.
 A spokesman for the group slammed the administrators for failing to consider the proposal, which he said the group would continue to push for.
 “Otherwise, as a result of the administrator’s decision today, the inevitable outcome of the Bain proposal is that investors are left with a very poor recovery, which is a manifestly unjust outcome,” he said.
 Lead administrator Vaughan Strawbridge told Guardian Australia the sale process, which kicked off eight weeks ago, had created “certainty of an outcome, not just for a sale but for the airline and for jobs”.
 He said between Bain and rival bidder Cyrus Capital Partners had been difficult and rejected allegations made by Cyrus that the administrators did not engage with it or return phone calls.
 To solve Virgin Australia’s immediate cash crisis, Bain has agreed to loan the airline $125m, which Strawbridge said should be enough to see the airline through until August, when creditors are due to meet.
 But creditors, who include the airline’s 10,000 staff and bondholders owed $2bn, will get no say in whether the sale process goes ahead.
 “We have agreed to sell the business, and we’ve got that power of sale,” Strawbridge said.
 “We’ve always said we were looking for binding agreements by the end of June, and that’s what we’ve done.
 “If we didn’t go down the path of a binding sale agreement then we wouldn’t achieve certainty of outcome for the airline or certainty of jobs.”
 He said creditors at the meeting would vote on whether the transaction was completed through a mechanism called a deed of company arrangement, but added: “it’s not the only way we can complete”.
 Strawbridge declined to say how much Bain would pay in total, how many jobs would be kept by the new  Virgin Australia or how much creditors owed $6.8bn would receive.
 The move came after rival bidder Cyrus Capital Partners dramatically quit the race on Friday morning, blasting the administrators for failing to engage with it and return phone calls.
  Cyrus said it delivered a bid last week but “since then, the administrators have not returned calls, emails, or meaningfully engaged with Cyrus to progress its offer”.
 It said the bid was followed up on Thursday with a sweetened package that would “increase the value of the transaction, improve the return to unsecured bondholders and deliver more certainty for the administrators”.
 “This too received no response other than an acknowledgement of receipt,” Cyrus said.
 “Despite the material improvements put forward, the administrators have still not engaged with Cyrus on its offer.”
 Strawbridge said Cyrus’s team were “a great group of people”.
 “We’ve really enjoyed working with them throughout this process, we’ve spent a lot of time with them,” he said.
 “All four of us administrators and our advisers spent more than two hours with them on Tuesday going through their business plan, going through their proposal.
  “I’m disappointed in their comments – I understand they’re upset, I totally get it.”
 The move also leaves out in the cold the bondholders, who this week lobbed a late proposal to tip $1bn into the airline and re-float it on the stock exchange.
 Strawbridge said this proposal had also been examined but it was highly conditional and non-binding.
  They said the deal “supports the current management team led by Paul Scurrah and their improvement plan for the airline”, indicating Scurrah will continue as chief executive for at least the short term.
 Virgin Australia collapsed into administration in April after coronavirus travel restrictions slashed passenger numbers and the Morrison government rebuffed its repeated requests for a bailout.
 “Bain Capital has presented a strong and compelling bid for the business that will secure the future of Australia’s second airline, thousands of employees and their families and ensure Australia continues to enjoy the benefits of a competitive aviation sector,” Strawbridge said.
 “From day one of our appointment, we have focused on achieving the best possible outcomes that a restructured, sustainable and profitable airline can deliver to all stakeholders.
 “In just over eight weeks, this is a very positive outcome. We have certainly been heartened by the levels of interest shown by parties, in spite of the prevailing covid-induced market conditions, how our final two groups have approached their bids, and how support for the business has come from so many quarters.”
 The selection of Bain as preferred bidder is a blow to unions, led by the Transport Workers Union, because the investment group plans a smaller airline than other bidders and has a track record of union-busting in the US.
 However, TWU national secretary Michael Kaine said that “Bain put forward a solid bid to secure the administrator’s recommendation and we are happy to work with them on the plan for getting the airline back on its feet”.
 He repeated his call for the Morrison government to aid the airline sector, which has been devastated by the coronavirus crisis, by introducing an “aviationkeeper” package to keep money flowing if jobkeeper payments to workers are cut off as planned in September.
  Virgin Australia’s bigger rival,  Qantas, slashed 6,000 jobs and announced a $1.9bn capital raising on Thursday, telling the market that international flights would not resume for at least a year.
 “Both Virgin and Qantas need financial assistance, support and direction from the government on weathering the difficult months ahead as air travel limps along,” Kaine said.
title: 维珍澳大利亚管理人员同意将航空公司出售给贝恩资本
title_en: Virgin Australia administrators agree to sell airline to Bain Capital
